risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "We used computer-generated randomization to assign new reviewers to either the intervention or control group." Comment: This explicitly states that allocation was generated by a computer randomization process, which meets the criterion for a truly random sequence.

Quote: "We used computer-generated randomization to assign new reviewers to either the intervention or control group." Comment: This reports the random sequence method, but it does not state how the assignment was concealed from the person enrolling participants or whether the recruiter could foresee the next allocation. No central allocation, SNOSE, identical containers, or other explicit concealment mechanism is described, so allocation concealment cannot be judged as low risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A senior editor emailed those in the intervention group and invited them to participate in a new mentoring program... Those in the control group received no initial study-specific contacts and no intervention beyond standard journal practices" and "The content and amount of communication were left to the mentor and mentee." Comment: Participants and personnel were not blinded to assignment in the intervention arm; the mentoring intervention was obvious to recipients and mentors. Because the main outcomes were editor-rated review quality and reviewers' experience/satisfaction, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence performance and ratings, so the study is at high risk of performance bias for this domain.

Quote: "All peer review at our journal is blinded" and "All reviews at Annals are routinely rated by editors for quality on a previously reported 5-point scale"; also, "Our main outcome measures were the mean review quality rating score for each reviewer, and the slope in change ... in editor-assigned reviewer quality ratings." Comment: The outcome assessor was the editor, who was not described as being blinded to intervention allocation; the primary outcome was a subjective/judgement-based editor rating of review quality, so knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence outcome measurement.

Quote: "Four subjects randomized to the mentorship group never made contact of any kind with their mentors, and thus did not receive the intended intervention. We therefore present below the per-protocol analysis excluding these four subjects; however we also performed a corresponding intention-to-treat analysis that yielded essentially identical results (data not shown)." Comment: Attrition was small (4 participants) and confined to the intervention arm, but the authors report an ITT analysis with essentially identical results, and the amount of missing/non-adherent data appears limited rather than likely to bias the outcome.
